---
output:
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    toc: false
    number_sections: false
documentclass: article
geometry: margin=1in
fontsize: 12pt
linestretch: 2
fig.cap: true
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup[table]{justification=raggedright,singlelinecheck=false}

latex_engine: xelatex

---

<!-- Title here: -->
**Simulation-Based Investigation of Variable Selection Methods**

<!-- Name here: -->
Mahfuza Haque Mahi

**Introduction:**

This project investigates the performance of five variable selection methods in multiple 
linear regression using a simulation study in which the true underlying model is known. 
The methods evaluated are: backward elimination based on F test and p-values, backward selection 
using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), backward selection using the Bayesian 
Information Criterion (BIC), the LASSO, and the Elastic Net. Simulated datasets will include 20 
continuous predictors, with five have predictors having true non-zero effects and the remaining fifteen having true coefficients of zero. Scenarios will vary both sample size and the degree of correlation among predictors. Performance will be evaluated along several dimensions: the ability of each method to correctly identify the five true predictors, its tendency to include extraneous variables, 
and the quality of the estimates it produces for retained variables in terms of bias, 
confidence interval coverage, Type I error, and Type II error. We expect that methods with stricter 
penalization will achieve lower false positive rates at the cost of higher Type II error, 
particularly for predictors with weaker true effects. We further expect that increasing 
predictor correlation will degrade performance across all methods, and that larger sample 
sizes will consistently improve selection accuracy and estimation precision.

**Preliminary Methods**

Data will be generated in R v4.3.1 using \texttt{gendata} 
from the \texttt{hdrm} package. Each dataset will include 20 
continuous predictors drawn from a multivariate normal distribution with mean zero and 
unit variance. The five truly important predictors (X1--X5) will have true coefficients of $0.5/3$, $1/3$, $1.5/3$, $2/3$, and $2.5/3$, respectively, and the 
remaining fifteen predictors (X6--X20) will have coefficients of zero. Errors will follow 
a standard normal distribution. An exchangeable correlation structure will be used with $\rho \in \{0, 0.35, 0.70\}$ across two sample sizes ($N = 250$ and $N = 500$), yielding six simulation scenarios. Before the full simulation, a large dataset ($N = 10{,}000$) will be generated to verify correlation structure via a correlation table.

Backward elimination for all three criterion-based methods will use the \texttt{step()} function applied to a full \texttt{lm()} model. The tuning parameter \texttt{k} will be set to \texttt{qchisq(0.85, df = 1)} for p-value selection ($\alpha = 0.15$), 
\texttt{k = 2} for AIC, and \texttt{k = log(n)} for BIC. LASSO and Elastic Net models 
will be fit using \texttt{cv.glmnet()} with standardized predictors and 10-fold 
cross-validation, with $\alpha = 1$ for LASSO and $\alpha = 0.5$ for Elastic Net. Two 
tuning parameters will be evaluated for each penalized method: \texttt{lambda.min} and 
\texttt{lambda.1se}. Because \texttt{glmnet} 
does not return standard errors, selected models will be refit under
\texttt{lm()} to obtain unpenalized coefficient estimates, confidence intervals, and 
p-values.  Any variable not selected is assigned a coefficient of zero and treated as non-significant.

Each of the six scenarios will be replicated 10{,}000 times, using a fixed master seed per 
scenario. Across replications, the 
following will be summarized for each method: true positive rate (proportion of 
simulations in which each of X1--X5 is selected), false positive rate (proportion of 
simulations in which any of X6--X20 is selected), bias (mean estimated minus true 
coefficient for X1--X5, using zero for non-selected variables), 95\% confidence interval 
coverage for X1--X5 (conditional on selection), Type I error (proportion of null 
predictors that are both selected and significant at $p < 0.05$ in the refitted model), 
and Type II error (proportion of replications in which an important predictor is either 
not selected or fails to reach $p < 0.05$ in the refitted model). Because inference after 
model selection is known to produce downward-biased standard errors and inflated Type I 
error, these results will be interpreted descriptively.
